exemplar.title: To what extent does the number of turns in a coil (50, 100, 150, 200) affect the strength of the magnetic field it produces, when varying voltages (1.5V, 3V, 4.5V, 6V, 9V, 12V) are applied across it?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does the number of turns in a coil (50, 100, 150, 200) affect the strength of the magnetic field it produces, when varying voltages (1.5V, 3V, 4.5V, 6V, 9V, 12V) are applied across it?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are clear, precise and wellâlabelled, with appropriate units and significantâfigure conventions.
Uncertainties for voltage, current and radius are recorded and a propagation formula applied.
Trendlines and visual comparisons of coil data sets enhance interpretative clarity.
weaknesses: The theoretical model omits number of turns N, introducing a major systematic inaccuracy.
Uncertainty propagation is not carried through to slope or final conclusions, and rounding is inconsistent.
Current uncertainties (Â±0.1 A) do not reflect realistic meter precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs communication of data recording and processing is both clear and precise, warranting a topâband mark for presentation. Consideration of uncertainties is evident but has important omissions, and a major conceptual flaw in the formula limits full accuracy. This places the analysis firmly in the 5â6 range, at the lower end of that band.

strengths: Research question is located in a specific, wellâdeveloped context with clear links to solenoids and practical applications.
Procedure is fully detailed (materials list, diagram, numbered steps), supporting reproducibility.
Controls and variables are thoroughly identified and justified, showing deep methodological understanding.
weaknesses: Minor omissions in specifying how coil diameter is maintained and measured.
Rationale for voltage range could be more quantitatively tied to theoretical limits or equipment constraints.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student situates the research question within a precise scientific and practical context, offers a fully reproducible methodology, and explains key controls. While there are small omissions (coilâdiameter monitoring, explicit voltageârange rationale), overall the design meets the highest band descriptor for clarity, specificity and reproducibility.

strengths: The conclusion accurately restates the observed linear relationship and aligns with AmpÃ¨reâs law.
Comparison to published studies and theoretical expectations situates findings in a scientific context.
Discussion reflects on both voltage and turnâcount effects, showing coherence with experimental aims.
weaknesses: Does not acknowledge the omission of the turnâcount factor in the model, limiting full justification.
Comparison to external literature is descriptive and lacks quantitative evaluation (e.g., percent deviations).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well supported by the data trends and connected to accepted theory, satisfying the top bandâs consistency requirement. The scientific context comparison is relevant, though somewhat superficial and lacking quantitative depth. Overall performance merits a high mark within the 5â6 range.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (no Gaussmeter, environmental effects, manual counting).
Proposes realistic and relevant improvements with rationales (use of Gaussmeter, environmental controls, automated counting).
Suggests forwardâlooking extensions (varying coil geometry, core materials, Faradayâs law applications).
weaknesses: Explanations of the magnitude of expected improvement could be more detailed (e.g., quantifying uncertainty reduction).
An empty evaluation table was referenced but not completed in the submission.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation comprehensively explains specific limitations and their relative impacts, and it offers realistic, wellâjustified improvements as well as meaningful extensions. This fully meets the highest band descriptor for explaining methodological weaknesses and suggesting improvements, justifying the maximum mark in this criterion.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student frames the research question clearly and embeds it within a focused discussion of solenoids, everyday generators, and specific household applications. This moves beyond a merely broad introduction and provides a concrete justification for studying coil turns and voltage, satisfying the descriptor for a âspecific and appropriate context.â Minor digressions about personal interest do not detract substantially from the contextual clarity, but the rationale is not so thorough or technical as to merit the very top of the range.

Uncertainties for voltage, current and radius are included and a propagation formula is applied, demonstrating active consideration. However, the fractional-error formula is mis-used (constant 0.1 A uncertainty is unrealistic) and no uncertainty is carried through to gradients or final conclusions. These significant omissions place the work in the âsome consideration with omissionsâ band.

Methodological considerations (control of core material, coil diameter, equipment consistency, safety) are described, and a variables table shows relevance to answering the question. However, the justification for why measuring current alone is sufficientâand the decision to calculate rather than directly measure the magnetic fieldâremains weak. Explanations of how each control secures data sufficiency are present but not deeply developed, matching the mid-band descriptor.

Specific weaknessesâabsence of a magnetic-field sensor, environmental factors, manual countingâare identified and briefly described, yet the explanation of their relative impact on results is limited to short notes. The work therefore meets the descriptor for describing, not explaining, methodological weaknesses.

A full material list, diagram, photograph, variables table and numbered procedure allow another student to replicate the data-collection process with only small ambiguities (e.g., precise method for keeping coil diameter constant, positioning of the ammeter, or duration of current flow). These omissions are not critical but introduce some uncertainty, aligning with âfew ambiguities or omissions.â

Processing is internally consistent but conceptually flawed: the formula for B omits the number of turns, so calculated field strengths do not truly reflect the physical quantity under investigation. This represents a major inaccuracy, though calculations follow the stated (incorrect) model. Hence the strand fits the 1-2 band for âmajor inaccuracies.â

Realistic improvements such as using a Gaussmeter, environmental control, or automated counting are proposed with concise rationales, but the explanations remain surface-level. The strand therefore satisfies ârelevant improvements are described,â consistent with the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Raw and processed data are organised in clearly labelled tables with units and uncertainties; graphs carry axis titles, units, and trendlines. Although formatting is occasionally untidy, the presentation is simultaneously clear (easy to follow) and precise (appropriate sig. figs. generally respected). This satisfies the top-band requirement for both clarity and precision.

A brief comparison is made to theoretical proportionality and to a published investigation, but discussion is superficial and lacks quantitative linkage or detailed literature evaluation. This equates to âsome relevant comparison,â placing the strand in the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

The student states a conclusion that mirrors the observed linear trends and ties it to AmpÃ¨reâs law, showing partial consistency with the analysed data. Because the underlying processing contains errors that are not recognised, the conclusion cannot be considered fully justified. This matches the descriptor âdescribed but not fully consistent.â

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated, but the student could strengthen the context by explicitly linking it to theoretical models (e.g., AmpÃ¨reâs law) in the introduction to justify the chosen voltages and turn counts.

The research question is well formulated and embedded within a focused context of solenoids and household generators, providing strong justification for investigating coil turns and magnetic field strength.

The Introductionâs anecdotal background engages the reader but lacks specific scientific context; consider adding a brief overview of relevant electromagnetism principles early to ground the investigation.
The student conveys personal motivation effectively and articulates why electromagnetism is compelling, adding genuine engagement to the research context.
The background on solenoids explains their function but could be enhanced by discussing how core material properties (permeability) quantitatively affect field strength.
Figure 1 is clearly labeled and effectively illustrates the solenoid configuration, aiding reader comprehension of the experimental setup.
The definitions of Ohmâs Law and AmpÃ¨reâs Law are presented clearly, establishing a solid theoretical foundation for the experiment.
In the formula $$B=\frac{I\mu_0}{2\pi r}$$ the number of turns, $$N$$, is omitted. Including $$N$$ (i.e., $$B=\frac{\mu_0NI}{2\pi r}$$) would directly link the theory to the research variable.
Table 1 lists independent variables well but does not justify why the selected voltages span 1.5âV to 12âV; a rationale for this range (e.g., coil heating limits) would aid reproducibility.
Table 2 of controlled variables is comprehensive, clearly identifying key factors and means of control, which supports high reproducibility.
Step 1 of the methodology omits how coil diameter is measured or kept uniform; specify how diameter will be monitored between windings to reduce variability.
The stepwise methodology (steps 1â8) is logically ordered and sufficiently detailed to allow replication, demonstrating strong procedural clarity.
Risk assessment addresses overheating and electric shock hazards effectively and outlines appropriate PPE and shutdown procedures.
The raw data table is clearly formatted but the current uncertainties are uniformly stated as Â±0.1âA, which may not reflect multimeter range accuracy; revisit error recording.
In propagating uncertainty for $$B$$, the student applies the quotient rule but fails to carry the resulting uncertainty through to gradient calculations; include propagated errors in slope analysis.
The processed data table includes numerical uncertainties but exhibits inconsistent rounding (e.g., 0.800 Â± 0.83 vs. 2.40 Â± 0.90); standardize significant figures.
Figure 3 presents voltage vs. magnetic field strength with distinct trendlines for each coil, offering clear visual comparison of the data sets.
The analysis states proportionality but does not quantify or compare the actual slopes and their uncertainties; include numerical slope values and confidence intervals for rigor.
The conclusion accurately restates that magnetic field strength increases with turns and voltage but does not acknowledge the theoretical omission of N in the model, limiting justification.
The comparison to an external investigation is a good start but remains descriptive; a quantitative literature comparison (e.g., % deviation) would strengthen scientific context.
The evaluation table identifies specific systematic and random errors and their sources, demonstrating insightful awareness of methodological limitations.
The proposed improvements are relevant and feasible; deepen explanations by discussing expected impact magnitudes (e.g., how much a Gaussmeter reduces uncertainty).
Section 12.2 on weaknesses and limitations references Table 7 but leaves it empty; ensure this table is completed with specific limitations and their impacts.
The proposed extensions explore coil geometry, materials, and Faradayâs Law applications, indicating strong forwardâthinking and relevance for further study.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

userSubmission.researchQuestion: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.
